steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks what the author would most likely agree with about Vancouver's "high number of pedestrian amenities." The passage's key claim is that no single factor "in isolation fully explains pedestrian habits in a given city" — so amenities are just one piece of a multi-factor picture.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• "It may increase walkability in Vancouver but is known to reduce walkability in other cities.": The passage never says amenities reduce walkability anywhere — this is unsupported. ✗
  • "It is better understood as an effect of the high level of pedestrian traffic in Vancouver than as a cause of that pedestrian traffic.": The passage does not discuss the causal direction between amenities and traffic at all. ✗
  • "It affects walking decisions in Vancouver less than demographic characteristics and average commuting distance do.": The passage lists demographics and commuting distance as examples of studied factors but never rank-orders them against amenities. ✗
  • "It should be understood as just one of several factors that influence pedestrian activity in Vancouver.": This directly matches the passage's conclusion that no single factor explains pedestrian habits — amenities are one of many. ✓
That matches the correct answer — D is correct.
